metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether S2 (`semantic.py`) is a hard landing prerequisite for S3, or whether S3 must also create/stub `semantic.py`; if S2 must land first, add an explicit 'Depends on S2 being closed' gate to the Scope section.
findings.finding: S2 (`src/rebar/grounding/semantic.py`) is absent from the codebase, yet the plan treats `semantic.refute_semantic`, `semantic.T2_BACKENDS`, and `semantic.available_backends()` as already-existing surfaces that S3 merely registers into. The epic's own signed manifest explicitly marks `src/rebar/grounding/semantic.py` as 'dep absent'. Every S3 AC that references these names (ACs 1, 2, 3, 4, 8) cannot be verified against the actual codebase.
findings.location: What section — 'registers `"pyright"` into `semantic.T2_BACKENDS` and `available_backends()`'; AC-1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Prerequisite: S2 (`src/rebar/grounding/semantic.py`) must be landed and merged before S3 begins. S3 imports `from rebar.grounding import semantic` and calls `semantic.T2_BACKENDS`, `semantic.available_backends()`, and `semantic.refute_semantic()` — none of which exist until S2 closes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `GroundingConfig` and `load_config` will expose `t2_enabled`, `t2_backend`, and `t2_timeout_seconds` before S3 lands (either confirm S2 adds them, or explicitly include their addition in S3's scope).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `GroundingConfig` (in `resolve.py`) already carries the three T2 config keys `t2_enabled`, `t2_backend`, `t2_timeout_seconds`, and that S3's pyright backend reads them via the existing `load_config`. The actual `GroundingConfig` dataclass has only `ctags_optlib_dirs`, `ctags_options`, `supported_languages` — the T2 keys are absent.
findings.location: What section — 'gated behind an optional extra plus `t2_enabled`'; 'the three `.rebar/grounding.toml` keys'; AC-2 ('With `t2_enabled=true, t2_backend="pyright"`')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `oracle.refute_absence`'s T2 escalation call-site (the S2 addition) is landed before S3's integration tests run, and add an explicit assertion in the test harness that `TIER_T2` is actually reached (not silently falling back to T1).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `oracle.refute_absence` already contains a T2 escalation call-site ('After the T1/deps lanes return … `oracle.refute_absence` calls `semantic.refute_semantic(…)`'). The actual `oracle.refute_absence` in `oracle.py` routes only T0 (deps) and T1 (ctags) — there is no T2 escalation hook, no import of `semantic`, and no call to `refute_semantic`.
findings.location: What section — 'A `refute(…) -> dict` that `semantic.refute_semantic` dispatches to when `t2_backend == "pyright"'"; "AC-2 ('a member/dotted reference that resolves … yields `refuted` at `TIER_T2`')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm S2 has landed the `*semantic.available_backends()` extension in `oracle._backend_availability()` before AC-8's test is authored; or explicitly add this extension to S3's scope if S2 does not cover it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `oracle._backend_availability()` already has a hook to append T2 backends via `*semantic.available_backends()`, and that S3 merely needs to register into it. The actual `_backend_availability()` is a hardcoded list of ctags/filesystem/opengrep/ast-grep/metric/sarif/registry entries with no T2 extension point.
findings.location: What section — 'registration into `semantic.T2_BACKENDS` + `available_backends()`'; AC-8 ('`grounding_info()` lists the pyright backend')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `reportMissingModuleSource` should be treated as an env-not-built signal (it fires for stub-less packages in valid envs); consider using only `reportMissingImports` as the env-built gate, or separating 'no stubs' from 'module not found' to avoid systematically abstaining on projects with C-extension dependencies.
findings.finding: The plan's `refuted@T2` precondition counts `reportMissingModuleSource` as an env-not-built signal, but this diagnostic fires legitimately in correctly-built Python environments when packages lack type stubs (e.g., most C-extension packages on PyPI). This would cause the backend to abstain with `other` (env-not-built detail) for many real projects even when the environment is fully built and semantic resolution is trustworthy — systematically undermining the backend's utility.
findings.location: What section — confirm-only mapping: `reportMissingImports` / `reportMissingModuleSource` = env built
findings.suggested_fix: Use only `reportMissingImports` (module not found on sys.path) as the env-not-built gate; `reportMissingModuleSource` (module found, stubs missing) should either be ignored or mapped to a narrower abstain that does not block `refuted` when it is the only import-resolution diagnostic present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the recognized rule-kind allowlist and message-matching strategy as a versioned, configurable artifact (e.g., a dict in `pyright_backend.py` with a clear update contract, or a `grounding.toml` key) so that pyright diagnostic rule changes can be addressed without touching core logic.
findings.finding: The diagnostic rule-kind allowlist (`reportAttributeAccessIssue`, `reportUndefinedVariable`, `reportMissingImports`, `reportMissingModuleSource`) and the leaf-name message-matching heuristic are described as hardcoded implementation details with no configuration or extension point. These rules are pyright-version-sensitive and will need updating as pyright evolves, making the backend brittle without a clear change path.
findings.location: src/rebar/grounding/pyright_backend.py — confirm-only mapping description under 'What'
findings.suggested_fix: Expose the rule-kind allowlist as a module-level constant (e.g., `REFUTE_RULES: frozenset[str]` and `ABSTAIN_ON_ENV_RULES: frozenset[str]`) with a docstring noting the pyright version they were validated against, and document in `docs/grounding.md` how to extend them when upgrading pyright.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan (or tighten the spec) that leaf-name substring matching is deliberately conservative and will produce false abstains for common names, OR consider using quoted-name matching (pyright messages typically quote the name: `"get"`) to reduce the false-abstain rate for generic leaf names.
findings.finding: The plan's mechanism for detecting whether a diagnostic 'concerns the reference' — matching by file + rule-kind + 'the leaf name appearing in the diagnostic message' via substring — is over-broad for common leaf names. For references like `os.path.join`, `dict.get`, `str.format`, `obj.set`, the leaf name (`join`, `get`, `format`, `set`) appears in many unrelated diagnostic messages, causing spurious abstains for those references even when they are fully valid.
findings.location: What section — confirm-only mapping: 'whose message names the reference's leaf name'
findings.suggested_fix: Match the leaf name as a quoted token in the diagnostic message (e.g. `f'"{leaf_name}"' in message`) rather than bare substring, since pyright consistently wraps identifiers in double-quotes in its diagnostic messages (e.g. 'Cannot access attribute "get" for class "Foo"'). This substantially reduces false abstains for common-word leaf names while maintaining the fail-safe property.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly where the cache dict is instantiated (e.g., in semantic.refute_semantic as a request-scoped local, or in oracle.refute_absence as a per-call dict passed through) and add an AC or test asserting pyright is invoked at most once per project root per review.
findings.finding: The 'caller-owned cache' for pyright JSON output has ambiguous placement: the plan specifies cache=None as a parameter to pyright_backend.refute(), but never specifies where the cache object is instantiated and threaded across calls within a single review, leaving the 'one invocation per review' invariant unenforceable.
findings.location: What section — 'New module src/rebar/grounding/pyright_backend.py': 'caches the parsed result in the caller-owned cache keyed by project root (one invocation per review, reused across references)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the exact calling convention from semantic.refute_semantic to pyright_backend.refute() (including whether config/GroundingConfig is passed) in this plan, or explicitly reference the S2 interface spec that defines it.
findings.finding: The plan references `semantic.T2_BACKENDS`, `semantic.refute_semantic`, and `semantic.available_backends()` as the dispatch interface pyright_backend.py registers into, but semantic.py does not exist in the codebase and its calling convention — specifically what arguments refute_semantic passes to the backend's refute() — is defined only in the epic description (ticket 850f), not in this plan.
findings.location: What section — 'registers into semantic.T2_BACKENDS + available_backends()'; AC1: 'registers "pyright" into semantic.T2_BACKENDS and available_backends()'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete, exhaustive closed set of pyright diagnostic rule codes that the backend treats as 'unresolved-kind' (routing to abstain), either as a named constant in pyright_backend.py or as an explicit table in the plan — do not rely on an open-ended example list.
findings.finding: The complete closed set of pyright diagnostic rule codes treated as 'unresolved-kind rules' (routing to abstain-at-reference rather than enabling refuted) is never enumerated — only examples are given (reportAttributeAccessIssue, reportUndefinedVariable, 'unresolved-import'), leaving an open-ended classification decision for the implementor.
findings.location: What section — 'Confirm-only mapping' bullet; also AC line: 'an unresolved-kind rule — reportAttributeAccessIssue / reportUndefinedVariable / unresolved-import'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_UNRESOLVED_RULES: frozenset[str]` constant to the plan's spec of pyright_backend.py, enumerating all rule codes (e.g. {"reportAttributeAccessIssue", "reportUndefinedVariable", "reportMissingImports", "reportMissingModuleSource", "reportOperatorIssue"}) plus a comment that any rule NOT in this set also routes to abstain (fail-safe catch-all).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact 'leaf name' extraction rule for dotted reference names (e.g. 'the last dot-separated segment of ref["name"]') and co-locate it with the confirm-only mapping decision protocol in the plan.
findings.finding: The 'leaf name' extraction rule for dotted references is undefined: the plan says the backend matches diagnostics 'whose message names the reference's leaf name' without specifying what the leaf of a dotted name like 'requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter' is.
findings.location: What section — 'Confirm-only mapping' bullet: 'whose message names the reference's leaf name'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: leaf = ref['name'].rsplit('.', 1)[-1] (the last segment), and note that the message-text match is case-sensitive substring search for this value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured log emission (e.g., `logger.warning`) for each non-trivial abstain path (timeout, parse_error, other/crash) so operators can observe and alert on backend failures.
findings.finding: New failure points (pyright timeout, subprocess crash, parse error) have no specified logging or instrumentation: the plan maps these to abstain closed-reasons but does not mention emitting any log/metric/trace so operators can observe and debug backend failures in production.
findings.location: src/rebar/grounding/pyright_backend.py — abstain mapping; Acceptance Criteria — fail-open matrix
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyright_backend.py`, emit a structured warning log (with `project_root`, `closed_reason`, and relevant exception/output snippet) for every non-`no_tool`/`unsupported_lang`/`ambiguous` abstain case, so a log aggregator can surface backend degradation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate AC4 and AC7 by either removing AC7 (the no_tool case is already covered by AC4's matrix) or clarifying what AC7 tests beyond AC4 (e.g., explicitly verifying no exception is raised, if that's the distinct intent).
findings.finding: AC7 ("A test with pyright removed from PATH shows the backend returns abstain(no_tool) without raising") is a near-duplicate of the `no_tool` case already listed in AC4's fail-open matrix ("pyright off PATH → no_tool"). Both ACs require the same test exercising the same scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 vs AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Remove AC7 and add 'no exception raised' as an explicit assertion requirement within AC4's fail-open matrix description, e.g.: 'Fail-open matrix — each case must return abstain with the stated closed reason AND must not raise: pyright off PATH → no_tool; ...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the informal 'unresolved-import' pseudo-rule with the correct pyright rule code(s) (verify against real pyright --outputjson output; likely reportMissingImports / reportMissingModuleSource already listed separately as the env-not-built guard).
findings.finding: 'unresolved-import' appears in the plan's rule-code vocabulary alongside camelCase pyright rule names, but is not a valid pyright --outputjson rule key — pyright uses 'reportMissingImports' and 'reportMissingModuleSource' for import resolution errors, not a kebab-case 'unresolved-import' string.
findings.location: What section — 'Confirm-only mapping': 'an unresolved-kind rule — reportAttributeAccessIssue / reportUndefinedVariable / unresolved-import'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 'unresolved-import' from the unresolved-kind rule list; the import-resolution guard is already handled by the env-not-built precondition check (reportMissingImports / reportMissingModuleSource → abstain(other)), so no separate 'unresolved-import' rule is needed in the diagnostic-at-reference check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Raise the `grounding-t2` version floor from `pyright>=1.1` to the first release that stabilised all relied-upon diagnostic rule codes (`reportAttributeAccessIssue`, `reportMissingImports`, `reportMissingModuleSource`, `reportUndefinedVariable`), and document this floor with the rationale in the `pyproject.toml` comment.
findings.finding: The `grounding-t2 = ["pyright>=1.1"]` version floor is an unverified capability assertion: pyright's `--outputjson` diagnostic rule vocabulary (specifically `reportMissingImports`, `reportMissingModuleSource`, `reportAttributeAccessIssue`, `reportUndefinedVariable`) has NOT been stable across the full `>=1.1` range. `reportAttributeAccessIssue` was introduced only in pyright ~1.1.240 (replacing older split rules); early 1.1.x releases use a different rule vocabulary. The confirm-only mapping is entirely keyed on these specific rule names, so accepting `pyright>=1.1` may silently accept installations where the expected rule codes don't exist or are named differently, causing the backend to mis-classify (abstain instead of refute, or map an unrecognized code incorrectly).
findings.location: What section — `grounding-t2 = ["pyright>=1.1"]` optional extra; AC line `pyproject.toml declares the grounding-t2 extra`
findings.suggested_fix: Set the floor to `pyright>=1.1.240` (the release that introduced `reportAttributeAccessIssue` as a unified rule) and add a comment: `# floor pins the stable diagnostic-rule vocabulary the T2 backend keys on (reportAttributeAccessIssue introduced ~1.1.240)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate tests/unit/grounding/test_oracle.py (specifically the three grounding_info schema-conformance tests) as affected consumers of the _backend_availability() change and confirm they are covered by 'pytest tests/unit/grounding/ exits 0'.
findings.finding: The existing test_oracle.py tests (test_contract_validates_against_grounding_info_schema, test_library_grounding_info_validates, test_cli_grounding_info_json_validates) are unenumerated consumers: they invoke oracle.contract() → _backend_availability(), which S3 modifies to append *semantic.available_backends(). These tests must pass after the change but are not listed in the plan's scope or AC.
findings.location: AC: 'pytest tests/unit/grounding/ exits 0' / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section: 'test_oracle.py's existing grounding_info schema-conformance tests are existing consumers of _backend_availability() and must remain green after the S3 change (no schema change needed — grounding_info.schema.json already accepts open backend names, confirmed by S2's conformance assertion).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale to the 'Why' section (or reference the epic's decision) explaining why pyright CLI-batch was chosen for this story: no long-lived server lifecycle, simplest fail-open, self-contained opt-in extra.
findings.finding: The S3 plan's 'Why' section gives only a functional rationale ('the seam is inert until a real backend plugs in') but does not state the positive design rationale for why pyright CLI-batch was chosen for THIS story — the rationale exists in the parent epic (850f) but is not reproduced or referenced. Per G6, a plan must state a positive rationale for the chosen approach.
findings.location: ## Why section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the What/Scope section: (a) identify the file/function that implements `grounding_info()` and describe what change is needed to surface the pyright backend's availability/version, and (b) clarify whether `grounding_info.schema.json` already exists, where it lives, and whether it needs updating for the new backend fields.
findings.finding: ACs 8 and 9 reference `grounding_info()` and `grounding_info.schema.json`, but the What/Scope sections say nothing about where `grounding_info()` lives, what existing implementation needs to change, or whether `grounding_info.schema.json` already exists and covers the new backend fields. An executor cannot implement or verify these ACs without stopping to investigate the codebase.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC 8 and AC 9; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add a bullet: 'Modify `src/rebar/grounding/semantic.py` (or wherever `grounding_info()` lives) to include pyright backend availability and version in its output. If `grounding_info.schema.json` exists, add the new backend fields; if not, create it. A test or CI step should run `python -c "import jsonschema; jsonschema.validate(grounding_info(), schema)"` to satisfy AC 9.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test (or make target) that calls `grounding_info()` after backend registration and validates the result against `grounding_info.schema.json`, and name it in the AC or test plan so it is clear how AC 9 is proven.
findings.finding: AC 9 ('grounding_info() output validates against grounding_info.schema.json after the change') has no concrete proving command specified. The plan lists `pytest tests/unit/grounding/` and `make lint` as terminal checks, but neither of these inherently validates JSON output against a schema file unless a test is written for it — and no such test is mentioned.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC 9
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `tests/unit/grounding/test_pyright_backend.py`: a test that calls `grounding_info()` with the pyright backend registered and asserts the output passes `jsonschema.validate(output, schema)` loaded from `grounding_info.schema.json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan entry (in What or Scope) describing whether `grounding_info.schema.json` pre-exists and whether the pyright backend's addition to `grounding_info()` output requires a schema update — and if so, make updating the schema an explicit deliverable.
findings.finding: AC9 requires `grounding_info()` output to validate against `grounding_info.schema.json`, but neither the schema file nor any validation step against it is described anywhere in the plan body (What, Scope, or deliverables). This is an orphan acceptance criterion with no backing deliverable, leaving the implementer with no guidance on whether/how to update or verify the schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC9: "`grounding_info()` output validates against `grounding_info.schema.json` after the change."
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'Update `grounding_info.schema.json` to accommodate the pyright backend entry (new `t2_backend` availability fields) and add a test asserting the post-change `grounding_info()` output validates against the updated schema.' Alternatively, if the schema already covers the new output without changes, note that explicitly ('the existing schema is forward-compatible; no update needed').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concurrency-safety contract for the project-root cache (e.g., a lock/asyncio.Lock guard, a future/promise pattern, or an explicit single-threaded invariant) so concurrent callers cannot both miss and both spawn pyright.
findings.finding: The caller-owned cache has no defined concurrency-safety contract: if multiple threads or coroutines invoke `refute_semantic` simultaneously for different references in the same project, two concurrent callers can both observe a cache miss and both launch `pyright --outputjson`, producing a TOCTOU race and redundant expensive subprocess executions.
findings.location: src/rebar/grounding/pyright_backend.py — `refute` description, cache paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Use a dict of `threading.Lock` (or `asyncio.Lock`) per project-root key: the first caller acquires the lock, runs pyright, and stores the result; subsequent callers block until the result is available, then read it. Document the threading model expected of the caller-owned cache.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the cache lifetime and retirement contract: specify when the caller should create a fresh cache (e.g., per-review-session only), whether there is a TTL or max-size bound, and document this expectation in the module docstring.
findings.finding: The cache retirement path is unspecified: the plan describes a 'caller-owned cache' but does not say when or how entries are evicted/cleared. If the cache object is held across multiple reviews or grows unboundedly (e.g., a long-running daemon reviewing many distinct project roots), it will accumulate stale pyright JSON and grow without bound.
findings.location: src/rebar/grounding/pyright_backend.py — `refute` description, cache paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Document that the cache dict must be freshly created per review session (not reused across independent reviews). Optionally add an `maxsize` parameter and use a `functools.lru_cache`-style bounded container to prevent unbounded growth if the caller misuses a long-lived cache.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step (in 'What' or a new Schema sub-item) that explicitly states whether `grounding_info.schema.json` requires updating to accommodate T2 backend entries, or confirms the schema is already open-ended (e.g. uses `additionalProperties: true` for the backends map), so the schema-validation AC is guaranteed to pass without a separate untracked change.
findings.finding: The AC requires `grounding_info().schema.json` to validate after the change, but the plan's implementation section makes no mention of whether or how `grounding_info.schema.json` needs to be updated to accommodate the new pyright backend fields (e.g. T2 backend availability/version entries). If the schema is not already extensible for new backend registrations, the AC will silently fail at test time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'grounding_info() output validates against grounding_info.schema.json after the change'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add: 'Update `grounding_info.schema.json` (if needed) to allow T2 backend entries in the `available_backends` map — or document that the schema's existing `additionalProperties` clause already accommodates them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirmed: `grounding-t2` extra is correctly absent and must be added by S3 as planned — no action needed beyond the existing AC-6.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `grounding-t2` optional extra exists in `pyproject.toml` (AC-6: '`pyproject.toml` declares the `grounding-t2` extra'). It does not — the file has `grounding`, `mcp`, `reviewbot`, `agents`, `eval`, `tracing`, `dev` extras, but no `grounding-t2`.
findings.location: AC-6 — '`pyproject.toml` declares the `grounding-t2` extra'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/grounding/__init__.py` to the plan's file impact list and specify whether `T2_BACKENDS` and `available_backends` should be re-exported at the package level.
findings.finding: src/rebar/grounding/__init__.py is an unenumerated edit target: it currently re-exports all public symbols from grounding sub-modules, and if S2's semantic.py adds T2_BACKENDS/available_backends those symbols will need to be re-exported (or deliberately excluded) — yet __init__.py does not appear in the plan's file impact list or Scope section.
findings.location: What section / Scope section — file impact list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether the dev extra should include grounding-t2 (or pyright directly) to satisfy 'pytest tests/unit/grounding/ exits 0' in CI, or confirm that all test assertions are fixture-only and pyright-off-PATH is exercised via the explicit no_tool matrix cell.
findings.finding: The dev extra in pyproject.toml does not include the grounding-t2 extra, so a fresh CI environment running 'pytest tests/unit/grounding/' without explicitly installing grounding-t2 will not have pyright available — the fail-open matrix fixtures requiring real pyright output will either be skipped or fail, contradicting AC 'pytest tests/unit/grounding/ exits 0'.
findings.location: AC: 'pytest tests/unit/grounding/ exits 0' / pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies]
findings.suggested_fix: Either add 'nava-rebar[grounding-t2]' to the dev extra in pyproject.toml, or explicitly state in the test design that the entire test suite uses captured --outputjson fixtures and PATH-manipulation for the no_tool cell, so pyright need not be installed.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
